summary: McKenzie describes reaching a point where his automated sales notification emails are becoming overwhelming due to increased sales volume. He receives three emails per transaction and is now getting enough sales (26 by the 20th of the month, approaching his best month of 30) that the notifications are interfering with his ability to spot actionable emails. He decides to turn off one of the three notifications, noting he'll reduce to one per transaction if sales increase another 25%. It's a brief, lighthearted milestone update about Bingo Card Creator's growing sales.
key_points: Sales volume has grown enough that three automated emails per transaction are becoming unmanageable
He's on pace to beat his best month ever (30 sales) with 26 sales by the 20th of the month
The practical problem of distinguishing actionable emails from informational ones emerges at even modest scale
money_quotes: I always had a brief catch in my breath when I saw Thunderbird downloading '1/3 mails on the server'.
Not that I mind every other email I received telling me that I had money, but it was getting harder to spot the mails which actually required action from the ones which were just for my reference.
So I turned off one of the three notifications. If my sales go up about 25% more, I'll be going down to one email per transaction.
key_insight: Even at micro-scale, growing sales force you to shift from savoring each individual transaction to building systems that let you focus on what requires action.
